preFix.title: Painting exterior render and masonry
preFix.subtitle: Apply masonry paint to outside walls in the correct sequence
preFix.excerpt: How to paint an outside wall of rendered masonry or brick: checking the render for defects, applying a masonry stabiliser to powdery areas, applying two coats of breathable masonry paint, and managing weather conditions.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: Exterior masonry paint protects rendered and brick walls from rain penetration and frost damage. A well-painted exterior wall can go 8 to 10 years before it needs attention. One painted carelessly over a poor surface will begin to flake within two seasons. The difference lies in preparation: the wall must be sound, dry. And free of algae before any paint is applied. And the correct grade of breathable masonry paint must be chosen for the substrate.

Do not paint in wet conditions or when the temperature is below 5°C. Frost will damage fresh masonry paint. Allow at least 3 dry days after heavy rain before starting; the render must be dry to depth. Not just on the surface. Check the forecast for 48 hours after your painting window as well.

Exterior masonry paint to match the existing finish. Masonry stabiliser for powdery walls. A 9-inch roller with a thick-pile sleeve for rough render. A 50 mm brush for cutting in, a scraper, and exterior-grade filler. For work above the first storey, arrange a scaffold tower or hired access platform.

Brush down the entire wall with a stiff brush to remove loose flakes, dirt, and algae. Treat any green algae or lichen with a proprietary fungicidal wash diluted to the manufacturer's instructions. Apply and leave for 24 hours before rinsing. Check the render for cracks or holes and fill with an exterior flexible filler or sand-and-cement patch. Allow repairs to cure for 4 days before painting over them.
Step 2, apply masonry stabiliser if needed

If the surface is powdery or chalky (wipe a hand across it; white powder means it needs stabilising). Apply masonry stabiliser diluted as directed and allow to dry fully. Skipping this step causes the top coat to lift the loose surface and peel.

Cut in around window frames, fascia boards, and at ground level with the brush first. Roll the main wall areas in overlapping horizontal strokes; rough textured render absorbs paint fast. So keep the roller well-loaded and maintain a wet edge to avoid lap marks. Allow the first coat to dry for 4 to 6 hours before the second. Two coats are the minimum; a wall that has not been painted for many years may need three.

attrs.fix: Strip the flaking area, allow the wall to dry fully, apply stabiliser, and repaint.
attrs.cause: Applied over a damp wall, or stabiliser was not applied over a powdery surface.
attrs.symptom: Paint flaking within months of application

attrs.fix: Always maintain a wet edge. Work in sections that can be completed and blended within 5 minutes.
attrs.cause: Each pass of the roller was allowed to dry before the adjacent pass was applied.
attrs.symptom: Streaky lap marks on the finished surface
